reasoning: The finding identifies missing esc_url() on admin_url() at line 195 of admin.php. Code inspection confirms: (1) admin_url() is called with a hardcoded string literal 'options-general.php?page=easy-google-fonts&screen=about' — there is no attacker-controlled input anywhere in the call chain. (2) The only theoretical exploit path cited is a WordPress filter hook (the 'admin_url' filter) modifying the return value to inject a javascript: or data: URI. Registering a WordPress filter requires code execution on the server — if an attacker can do that, they have already achieved RCE and esc_url() provides zero additional protection. (3) The function add_help_tabs() is hooked via 'load-settings_page_easy-google-fonts', which only fires for authenticated admins viewing the plugin settings screen. (4) The other two href attributes on lines 196-197 use hardcoded https:// URLs and are clearly safe. This is a WordPress Coding Standards (WPCS) style violation — missing esc_url() around admin_url() — not an exploitable vulnerability. The sink is not reachable with attacker-controlled input under any realistic threat model. Marking is_real=false.
